content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 25,
1992
Honorable James C.
Richards
City Counci lmember
City of San Jacinto
Post Office Box 488
San Jacinto,
CA
92383
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—146
Dear Councilmember Richards:
This letter is intended to confirm the advice that was
provided to you by telephone on March 17,
1992,
concerning your
duties as a San Jacinto City Councilmember pursuant to the
conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”).
1
Please note that this letter is based on the facts
presented to us.
The Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogeisby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71..)
QUESTION
May you participate in city council decisions in which Roger
Morgan or any of his companies are either directly or indirectly
involved?
CONCLUSION
From the facts you have provided, it would appear that
neither Roger Morgan nor any of his companies are a “source of
income” to you.
Consequently,
absent some other disqualifying
economic interest as set forth in Section 87103,
or some direct
financial effect on you personally resulting from governmental
decisions involving Roger Morgan or any of his companies,
you may
participate in such city council decisions.
-
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
re to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807
*
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-146
Page
2
FACTS
You are a broadcast consultant.
You currently own and manage
a media consulting firm, Jimco Broadcast Consultants.
In that
capacity you handle broadcast property for sale,
communications
sites for lease,
and advertising, promotions and marketing for
individual clients.
During 1990,
you had a contract with KESQ-TV in Palm Springs
to locate a site where they would be able to lease land to build a
television translator to serve the cities of Hemet and San
Jacinto.
Roger Morgan and one of his companies,
Inter—Mac,
owned
an antenna farm
(communications site)
located just outside the
city limits.
On January 15,
1991,
you set up a meeting with
various city officials, television station representatives,
and
Roger Morgan.
You and Roger Morgan also did a signal search at
the site.
During your conversations with Roger Morgan in March,
1991,
Roger Morgan told you that he would consider selling the
communications site.
He asked you to contact some of your clients
to see if they would be interested.
The oral agreement that
existed between Mr.
Morgan and yourself was that in the event you
sold the property,
you would receive a
5 percent finder’s fee.
The finder’s fee would be paid by the buyer.
You contacted at
least 10 potential buyers directly for this purpose,
none of whom
was the buyer who eventually purchased Mr.
Morgan’s communications
site.
At no time did you have a written contract to act as an agent
for Mr.
Morgan nor did you hold a listing on the subject property.
Mr.
Morgan has never been client of yours and has never provided
income to you or your company.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that
an official has a financial interest if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immedjate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(C)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on


Our File No.
1-92-146
Page
3
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
Section 87103.
In Section 87103(c), the term “source of income” includes not
only the source of income which has been received by the public
official, but also the source of income which has been promised to
the official.
The term “promise” is defined,
in part,
as a
“[r)easonable ground for hope or expectation....”
(Funk
&
wagnalls Standard Co1lee Oictionarv
(1974)
p.
1078.)
We have had occasion to apply this concept of “promised”
income in several situations.
For example,
we have advised that a
real estate agent who had a single listing from a seller was not
“promised”
income by the seller until he had secured a buyer.
This was because prior to that time the expectation of receipt of
income was considered to be too speculative to rise to the level
of “promised” income.
(Remelmever Advice Letter,
No. A-81-510.)
However,
in a similar but distinct situation,
we have advised that
a real estate agent who had multiple listings from a seller had a
strong prospect of future income from this client.
(Felts Advice
Letter,
No.
A—85—130.)
This was considered to be “promised”
income.
From the facts you have provided, not only have you never
received any income from Roger Morgan or any of his companies,
but
you have never held a listing on the communications site.
Thus,
it would appear that neither Roger Morgan nor any of his companies
are a “source of income” to you.
Consequently,
absent some other disqualifying economic
.
4
terest as set forth in Section 87103,
or some direct financial
effect on you personally resulting from governmental decisions
involving Roger Morgan or any of his companies,
you may
participate in such city council decisions.


Our File No.
1—92-146
Page
4
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested.
If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.2
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
,2&tAtVti >IL
By:
Deanne Stone
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:DRS:aa
2
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1Oc per page.
